trajectory_summary: The call opens with a cost-skeptic customer who has already talked himself out of solar, and closes with a booked Tuesday appointment and Jim asking unsolicited questions about offset coverage. Holland built real energy here — the trajectory is genuinely positive. The primary gap is that the win was built on an incentives reframe of uncertain accuracy rather than on bill pain, which means the closer inherits a customer whose enthusiasm is anchored to claims Holland couldn't fully substantiate.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is 'unknown' — this call should be tagged 'appointment_booked'. Jim confirmed Tuesday at 5PM, acknowledged the computer requirement, and ended the call asking product questions. The appointment has structural fragility (pre-closing on the home, no bill captured, incentives claims unverified) but it is a booked sit, not a tentative or callback.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Incentives accuracy — know what you can name before you name it
primary_coaching_focus.quote: So we've been able to get a lot of new incentives such as state incentives, veteran incentives, stuff like that.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland's reframe move was correct and effective, but the specific incentives cited (state, veteran) were ungrounded. Jim has no competing quote to check these against right now, but the closer will walk into a customer expecting incentives Holland couldn't substantiate. Manager should use this moment to establish what Holland actually knows about Ion's current incentive stack — and where the line is between 'the specialist will walk you through the full picture' and improvising specifics that may not hold.

key_moments.quote: When if you don't mind me asking, when was the last time that you saw, like, an estimate for your home, if you have?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland executes verify.prior_designs cleanly and surfaces a critical fact: Jim has never seen a solar design. He's working from a generic $20K figure he encountered somewhere, not from a real proposal. This is a first-look customer with zero competitive anchoring — the closer walks in with a clean slate and no competing quote to beat.

key_moments.quote: yeah. The only incentive actually that was removed was the federal tax credit, which does actually sound like something that you're interested in. But, obviously, like, that's the biggest thing when it comes to solar was that incentive. So we knew that that would be an issue. Right? So we've been able to get a lot of new incentives such as state incentives, veteran incentives, stuff like that.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's incentives reframe is strategically correct — interrupt the 'incentives are gone' exit ramp — but the execution introduces unverified specifics. The codex's tax credit disclosure says Ion 'can no longer guarantee the Tax Credit on purchased systems' and that purchase options exist without it. Holland goes further, claiming 'state incentives' and 'veteran incentives' without grounding. Jim has no competing quote to check these against, which reduces immediate risk, but the closer will inherit a customer expecting incentives Holland couldn't name.

key_moments.quote: The only thing actually that you need to qualify for those is just that you have a credit score above 670. So do you know if that would be the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland deploys the credit qualifier correctly and at the right moment — immediately after the incentives reframe, so Jim's 'yes' to the credit question functions as a double confirmation: he qualifies AND the incentives apply to him. This is positive execution. Holland uses Jim's confirmation ('Okay. Then yeah. No. I actually think that it would be it would work out pretty well for you') to pivot from skepticism to forward momentum. The threshold stated (670) matches the codex.

key_moments.quote: Yes. And, actually so I'm closing on this house this coming Monday, so it's not mine yet. All indication is that most everything is signed, so I I should own it soon. But that is one of the things when I do own it is I I will take a look at those trees, not just for the solar, but just for the, because I don't want those limbs hanging over my house. What about Yeah. Do you see on the Google Maps? What about on the garage? Is that surface area that we could use too?
key_moments.stewart_read: Jim discloses three things in one breath: (1) he's closing Monday — the appointment is Tuesday, meaning he'll be a homeowner by the time the closer calls; (2) he's already planning to remove the trees that Holland flagged as a shading concern — improving solar viability without prompting; (3) he proactively asks about the garage roof as additional panel surface. This is a customer co-designing the install before the appointment exists. Holland catches the garage angle well ('I actually want to say that that might be a better place for the panels than even your home roof') but doesn't explicitly anchor the tree-removal signal as a positive qualifier.
